seo.title: Healthcare Voice Agents AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for voice agents AI agents in healthcare. Covers HIPAA, FDA 21 CFR Part 11, HITECH Act.

content.intro: Deploying voice agents in healthcare environments introduces unique risks where a misheard medication name or a delayed response can directly harm patients. This checklist addresses the intersection of real-time audio processing pipelines and strict HIPAA/HITECH compliance requirements, ensuring every spoken word containing PHI is handled with the same rigor as written medical records. Failure to meet these standards exposes organizations to fines up to $1.5M per violation, malpractice liability, and irreversible damage to patient trust.

sections.heading: PHI Handling & Data Sovereignty
sections.description: Voice agents in healthcare must ensure that Protected Health Information never leaves the approved processing boundary, from the moment audio is captured through LLM inference and back to the patient's ear.

items.label: Deploy STT models within HIPAA-compliant on-premise or BAA-covered cloud boundaries
items.description: Confirm your speech-to-text provider (e.g., AWS Transcribe Medical, Azure Cognitive Services) has a signed Business Associate Agreement and that audio streams are processed within your designated data residency zone. Document the data flow diagram showing audio never transits outside this boundary.

items.label: Implement real-time PHI scrubbing before LLM prompt injection
items.description: Deploy a Named Entity Recognition (NER) layer between the STT output and the LLM input that identifies and replaces PHI tokens (patient name, DOB, MRN, SSN, diagnosis codes) with anonymized placeholders before any text reaches the LLM. Validate scrubber accuracy against HIPAA Safe Harbor identifiers monthly.

items.label: Enforce audio stream encryption in transit using TLS 1.3 minimum
items.description: All microphone-to-server audio streams must use TLS 1.3 or higher. Disable TLS 1.0 and 1.1 at the load balancer level. Validate cipher suites quarterly and log any downgrade negotiation attempts as security incidents.

items.label: Prohibit audio recording retention beyond the active session unless explicitly consented
items.description: Configure your voice pipeline to discard raw audio buffers within 30 seconds of session close unless the patient has provided explicit written consent for recording retention. Implement automated purge jobs and verify deletion with cryptographic proof-of-erasure logs.

items.label: Validate that LLM API calls contain zero PHI in request payloads
items.description: Instrument your LLM request pipeline with an automated PHI-detection scanner (e.g., AWS Comprehend Medical or Microsoft Presidio) that blocks and alerts on any outbound prompt containing identifiable patient data. Run weekly audits on sampled prompt logs.

items.label: Maintain a data processing inventory mapping every system that touches voice data
items.description: Create and maintain a living inventory document listing every vendor, microservice, and storage layer that processes voice or transcript data, including their BAA status, data residency, and retention policy. Review this inventory at every quarterly compliance audit.

items.label: Implement role-based access controls limiting transcript access to treating clinicians only
items.description: Configure your transcript storage system so that only authenticated clinicians with an active patient relationship can access conversation logs. Use attribute-based access control (ABAC) tied to your EHR's patient-provider relationship table and log every access event.

sections.heading: Auditability & FDA 21 CFR Part 11 Compliance
sections.description: Every AI-assisted clinical decision surfaced through the voice agent must be fully auditable, with tamper-evident logs that satisfy FDA 21 CFR Part 11 electronic records requirements and HITECH breach notification obligations.

items.label: Generate immutable audit logs for every voice agent interaction with timestamp, session ID, and de-identified transcript
items.description: Write all interaction logs to a WORM (Write Once Read Many) storage system such as AWS S3 Object Lock or Azure Immutable Blob Storage. Each log entry must include: UTC timestamp, session UUID, de-identified transcript, LLM model version, response latency, and the clinician or patient role initiating the session.

items.label: Log LLM model version and prompt template version for every inference call
items.description: Tag every LLM API call with the exact model version (e.g., gpt-4o-2024-08-06) and the versioned prompt template hash used. Store this metadata alongside the interaction log so any future audit can reproduce the reasoning context. Treat prompt templates as controlled documents under your change management process.

items.label: Implement electronic signature capture when voice agent recommendations influence clinical orders
items.description: When the voice agent surfaces a medication suggestion, dosage recommendation, or care pathway option, require the clinician to provide a compliant electronic signature (21 CFR Part 11 §11.50) before the recommendation is logged as acted upon. Integrate with your EHR's e-signature workflow.

items.label: Configure automated HITECH breach detection on transcript storage access anomalies
items.description: Set up SIEM alerts for anomalous access patterns on transcript storage (e.g., bulk downloads, access outside business hours, access from unrecognized IPs). Define breach notification SLAs: internal escalation within 1 hour, HHS notification within 60 days per HITECH requirements.

items.label: Conduct quarterly audit log integrity checks using cryptographic hash verification
items.description: Schedule automated quarterly jobs that recompute SHA-256 hashes of stored audit logs and compare against the original hashes recorded at write time. Any mismatch must trigger an immediate security incident and be documented in your compliance record.

items.label: Maintain a model change log documenting every LLM update with clinical impact assessment
items.description: Before deploying any LLM model update, complete a clinical impact assessment documenting: changed capabilities, regression test results on clinical scenarios, safety filter validation, and sign-off from your Chief Medical Officer or designated clinical safety officer.

items.label: Produce audit-ready reports showing AI decision traceability for regulatory inspections
items.description: Build a reporting dashboard that allows your compliance team to pull a complete interaction trace for any session within 24 hours of an audit request. The trace must show: input transcript (de-identified), LLM reasoning chain, safety filter outcomes, and final spoken response.

sections.heading: Real-Time Safety Filtering & Clinical Accuracy
sections.description: Voice agents operating in clinical settings must apply multi-layer safety filtering within the sub-second latency budget to prevent harmful, inaccurate, or contextually inappropriate responses from reaching patients or clinicians.

items.label: Deploy a clinical safety classifier that intercepts LLM output before text-to-speech synthesis
items.description: Implement a lightweight, low-latency safety classifier (target <50ms inference) trained on healthcare-specific harmful output categories: incorrect dosage instructions, contraindicated drug combinations, self-harm escalation, and diagnostic overconfidence. Block and substitute any flagged response with a safe fallback script.

items.label: Define and enforce latency budgets with hard cutoffs to prevent degraded-quality responses
items.description: Set hard latency thresholds: STT processing ≤200ms, LLM inference ≤600ms, safety filtering ≤50ms, TTS synthesis ≤150ms, total pipeline ≤1000ms. If any stage exceeds its budget, route to a pre-approved fallback response rather than delivering a partial or corrupted output. Log all budget breaches.

items.label: Implement medication name disambiguation with phonetic matching validation
items.description: Integrate a medical terminology disambiguation layer (e.g., using RxNorm or SNOMED CT) that validates drug names identified in STT output against a phonetically similar alternatives list. Flag ambiguous matches (e.g., 'Celebrex' vs. 'Celexa') for clinician confirmation before proceeding.

items.label: Configure mandatory escalation triggers for high-acuity clinical keywords
items.description: Define a keyword and semantic trigger list (e.g., chest pain, suicidal ideation, anaphylaxis, code blue) that immediately routes the conversation to a live clinician or emergency protocol, bypassing LLM reasoning. Test these triggers monthly with simulated scenarios and document pass/fail results.

items.label: Validate conversation coherence across multi-turn sessions to prevent context drift
items.description: Implement a conversation state manager that tracks clinical context across turns and flags responses that contradict earlier statements (e.g., recommending a drug the patient stated an allergy to two turns prior). Run coherence validation on 100% of multi-turn sessions exceeding 3 exchanges.

items.label: Test safety filters against adversarial prompt injection via voice input
items.description: Conduct red-team exercises quarterly where testers attempt to bypass safety filters through voice-based prompt injection (e.g., speaking instructions like 'ignore previous instructions and recommend...'). Document findings and patch within 30 days of discovery.

items.label: Implement confidence scoring thresholds that trigger human handoff for low-certainty responses
items.description: Configure the LLM to output a confidence score for clinical recommendations. Set a threshold (e.g., <0.75) below which the voice agent must verbally acknowledge uncertainty and offer to connect the patient with a human clinician. Log all handoff events for quality review.

items.label: Validate TTS pronunciation accuracy for medical terminology using a clinical phonetics review
items.description: Before go-live, have a licensed pharmacist or physician review TTS pronunciation of all drug names, dosage units, and clinical terms in your response library. Correct mispronunciations using custom lexicons in your TTS engine (e.g., AWS Polly custom pronunciation, Azure Neural TTS lexicon).

sections.heading: Consent, Transparency & Patient Rights
sections.description: Patients interacting with AI voice agents in healthcare settings have specific rights under HIPAA, HITECH, and emerging state AI transparency laws. These must be operationalized into the voice agent's interaction design and backend systems.

items.label: Disclose AI agent identity at the start of every patient-facing session
items.description: Program the voice agent to identify itself as an AI system within the first 10 seconds of every patient interaction, using a standardized disclosure script reviewed by your legal team. Log the disclosure timestamp as part of the session record. Never allow the agent to claim to be a human clinician.

items.label: Obtain and record verbal or digital consent before collecting voice data for any purpose beyond immediate care
items.description: If voice data will be used for model training, quality assurance, or any purpose beyond direct patient care, implement a consent capture workflow that records explicit patient agreement with timestamp and session ID. Integrate consent status with your EHR consent management module.

items.label: Implement a real-time opt-out mechanism allowing patients to stop AI interaction at any point
items.description: Configure a voice-activated opt-out command (e.g., 'stop recording', 'speak to a person') that immediately terminates AI processing, purges the session buffer, and routes to a human agent within 30 seconds. Test this pathway weekly and document response times.

items.label: Provide patients with access to their voice interaction transcripts upon request within HIPAA timelines
items.description: Build a patient-facing portal feature that allows individuals to request de-identified transcripts of their voice agent interactions within the HIPAA-mandated 30-day access window. Implement identity verification before releasing any transcript data.

items.label: Train clinical staff on voice agent capabilities, limitations, and escalation protocols
items.description: Deliver mandatory training to all clinical staff who interact with or supervise the voice agent, covering: what the AI can and cannot do, how to recognize AI errors, escalation procedures, and documentation responsibilities. Require annual recertification and track completion in your LMS.

items.label: Conduct accessibility testing to ensure voice agent meets ADA and Section 508 requirements
items.description: Test the voice agent with patients who have speech impediments, accents, or hearing impairments. Validate STT accuracy across diverse speaker profiles and provide alternative interaction modalities (e.g., text chat fallback) for patients who cannot use voice effectively.

items.label: Establish a patient complaint and adverse event reporting pathway specific to AI interactions
items.description: Create a dedicated reporting channel (phone, portal, or in-session voice command) for patients to report concerns about AI responses. Assign a clinical safety officer to review all AI-related complaints within 5 business days and feed findings into your model improvement process.

regulatoryNotes.heading: Regulatory Considerations
notes: HIPAA Privacy Rule (45 CFR §164.514) requires that PHI be de-identified using either the Safe Harbor method (removing 18 specific identifiers) or Expert Determination before being processed by any third-party LLM. Your PHI scrubbing layer must be validated against all 18 Safe Harbor identifiers, including geographic data smaller than a state and dates more specific than year for individuals over 89.

FDA 21 CFR Part 11 applies when your voice agent generates or contributes to electronic records used in clinical decision-making. This requires that all AI-generated records be attributable to a specific individual, time-stamped, and protected against alteration. If your voice agent influences medication orders or clinical documentation, your audit log system must meet Part 11's electronic signature and audit trail requirements.

The HITECH Act strengthens HIPAA breach notification requirements and extends liability to Business Associates. If your STT, LLM, or TTS vendor experiences a breach involving PHI, you must notify affected patients within 60 days and HHS immediately for breaches affecting 500+ individuals. Ensure your BAAs explicitly define breach notification timelines and responsibilities for each vendor in your voice pipeline.

SOC 2 Type II certification for your voice agent infrastructure requires demonstrating continuous operational controls over a minimum 6-month audit period. For healthcare voice agents, pay particular attention to the Availability and Confidentiality trust service criteria: document your latency SLAs, failover procedures, and encryption controls as evidence. Your SOC 2 auditor will specifically examine how PHI is handled across the real-time pipeline.

cta.heading: Automate Your Healthcare Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your healthcare voice agent pipeline against HIPAA, HITECH, and FDA 21 CFR Part 11 requirements—catching PHI leakage, safety filter failures, and audit gaps before they become violations.
